Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe sit down when I felt like it, which wasn't often. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep or when guilt finally caught up with me. With 16 online courses, I was constantly behind, so organizing anything felt pointless. I'd just pick whatever seemed most urgent and half-heartedly work through it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving, and even then I'd often miss them anyway. Some weeks I'd do nothing, other weeks I'd cram for hours trying to catch up. Regular study times? That's not really my thing. Life stuff and my own lack of motivation made any consistency impossible.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification on my phone or pure boredom that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd check just to see how far behind I was, which usually made me feel worse. Occasionally the gamified tasks would pop up and I'd think maybe I could earn some points or whatever. But most of the time, logging in felt like a chore I kept putting off.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The LMS just felt like another thing demanding my attention, and I already had too much going on in my head. I never got to that point where checking it felt natural or automatic. It always required effort I didn't have.
Q5: The gamified tasks were probably the only things that kept me somewhat engaged. They made the work feel less boring, like there was at least some point to doing it beyond just grades. The videos were okay too when I actually watched them, since I learn better by doing and seeing things in action. But honestly, nothing felt super useful because I was too disengaged to really absorb much.
Q6: The discussion forums I basically ignored completely. I posted maybe five times the whole course, and those were probably one-word responses. Reading other people's posts felt tedious and I couldn't bring myself to care about academic discussions. The lengthy reading materials I'd skip too because staring at screens for that long made my anxiety worse. I just didn't have the energy.
Q7: If something was quick and had some kind of immediate payoff, I'd do it. The gamified elements helped because at least I felt like I was accomplishing something small. Anything that dragged on or felt abstract and disconnected from real application, I'd zone out. I need to feel like my time is actually producing something tangible, not just ticking boxes.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff might as well not exist for me. If it's not required, my brain immediately files it under 'skip.' Required activities at least had the threat of consequences, which sometimes pushed me to do them. But even then, I'd often calculate the minimum I could get away with. Optional activities felt like extra work I definitely wasn't signing up for.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download PDFs and videos so I could look at them later without internet issues. Sometimes I'd just watch YouTube videos on the same topics because they explained things better or faster. I also learned a lot just by talking through concepts with a friend who was in a similar program. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be thinking through problems while doing other stuff, like working or commuting. I'd have downloaded notes on my phone and read through them on the bus. Or I'd be watching a video I'd saved offline while eating. The LMS only sees when I'm actively clicking around, not when I'm actually processing information in my own time.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a video once, download it or screenshot the key parts, and never go back to the LMS for it. My internet isn't always reliable and data is expensive, so downloading made more sense. I'd study from those saved files for days, but the system would just show one brief login. It's more practical for me that way.
Q12: Around midterms everything got harder. My anxiety was through the roof and I just couldn't focus on anything. I felt overwhelmed by how many courses I was juggling and how behind I'd fallen. There were days I'd open the LMS, stare at the screen, and close it without doing anything because it all felt too much. Boredom mixed with anxiety is a weird combination that just paralyzes you.
Q13: Absolutely. I was constantly exhausted from stress and other responsibilities outside school. When I was tired or anxious, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. I'd log in, see the mountain of incomplete tasks, and immediately feel worse. So I'd log out and try to distract myself with something else. It became a cycle of avoidance.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have videos playing in the background while I was doing something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. My mind would be somewhere else, worrying about other things. The LMS probably recorded that I spent time on stuff, but I wasn't really there mentally.
Q15: All the time. Assignments especially. I knew they mattered but starting them felt impossible. The anxiety of potentially failing made me avoid them even more, which is stupid but that's how my brain works. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. By then it was often too late to submit anything decent.
Q16: Deadlines were the only real motivator, but they also stressed me out massively. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was basically breathing down my neck, then panic and rush through whatever I could. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because I'd frozen up. The pressure helped sometimes, but mostly it just made my anxiety worse and my work sloppier.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly. A decent grade would make me think maybe I could do this. But negative feedback or low scores just confirmed what I already felt, that I wasn't cut out for this. I'd disengage even more after bad feedback. It's not a great response but that's honestly how it affected me.
Q18: The course structure was pretty overwhelming with so much content spread across so many modules. I never felt like I knew where to start or what to prioritize. The gamified elements helped a bit by giving me smaller goals, but overall the structure felt like it was designed for people who were already organized and motivated. That's not me.
Q19: The sheer volume of content made engagement harder. Having 16 courses meant nothing ever got my full attention. The gamification helped make some tasks feel less boring, which was good. But the lack of hands-on, practical activities hurt because I learn by doing things physically, not just reading or watching. More interactive stuff would've helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I can totally relate to that. You log in, click around, maybe watch some videos or read some stuff, but when it comes to actually producing work, there's this wall. For me it's anxiety and not knowing where to start. You can spend hours in the system without actually completing anything because you're just paralyzed or going through the motions.
Q21: That's basically me. My exam score was decent even though I barely touched the LMS compared to others. I studied in ways that didn't show up, used external resources, talked to people. The LMS isn't the only way to learn. Some of us just engage differently, and the system doesn't capture that at all.
Q22: Course logs can show when you click things and how long you're technically on a page. But they can't tell if you're actually paying attention or just have the tab open. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the discussions with friends. They show activity, not actual learning or understanding. It's a pretty incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just get through it. It's when you're curious and thinking about the material even when you're not studying. For me, I rarely felt that this semester. I was mostly just trying to survive and not fail, which isn't the same as being engaged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I dealt with every time I thought about coursework. It wouldn't capture the offline studying I did or the mental energy I spent just trying to motivate myself to start. It also wouldn't show that despite seeming disengaged, I actually understood more than my activity suggested. The numbers don't tell the whole story of what's going on with someone.
